title: Circuit Design Review Assistant
tagline: An AI that flags design risks in a BOM or schematic description — without ever inventing a number it didn't get from you.

levels.label: Junior
levels.intro: Get it working: setup, persona, structured output, error handling.
levels.theory: A circuit design review assistant takes a description of a circuit — a bill of materials (BOM), a netlist summary, or a plain-text description of components and ratings — and flags design risks: missing protection components, mismatched voltage/current ratings, unclear grounding, and so on. The system prompt must scope the model strictly to reviewing what it's given: it flags issues in the supplied design, it does not invent component values, perform real electrical calculations it can't show the working for, or assert a design is 'safe' — only that it didn't find a specific, named issue. Use genai.Client(api_key=...) with the key loaded via load_dotenv(), never hardcoded. Request structured output (a list of findings, each with a severity and a specific reason) rather than free-text prose — an engineer scanning review output needs to triage findings, not parse a paragraph. Wrap every call in error handling so a malformed response produces a visible 'review failed, try again' state rather than a silently empty findings list that reads as 'no issues found'.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Junior theory — specifically why an empty findings list and a failed API call must never look the same to the engineer reading it.

levels.label: Senior
levels.intro: Make it efficient: timeouts, retries, schema validation, careful caching.
levels.theory: Unlike a generic chatbot, caching here is conditionally fine: the same exact BOM submitted twice should produce the same review, so caching by a hash of the full design input is safe — what's unsafe is caching by a fuzzy or partial match, since two designs that look similar can differ in exactly the detail that matters (a single component's voltage rating). Efficiency levers: (1) Timeouts on every call, since a hung review blocks an engineer mid-design-iteration. (2) Retry with backoff only on transient failures (timeouts, 5xx) — never retry on a validation failure, since that means the model's output itself was malformed, not the network. (3) Schema validation before any finding is shown — every finding must include which specific component or line it refers to; a finding with no specific reference is not useful and should be rejected, not displayed.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Senior theory on what makes caching safe here versus dangerous in the clinical build — the difference is exact-match versus fuzzy-match.

levels.label: Architect
levels.intro: Make it safe: no hallucinated calculations, confidence levels, audit trail.
levels.theory: Three architect-level risks specific to engineering review tools. (1) Hallucinated numbers — an LLM can produce a confident-sounding voltage drop, current draw, or thermal calculation that was never actually computed. The system prompt must forbid presenting any calculated value unless the working is shown step by step, and even then the output should be labelled as a model estimate, not a verified calculation — real load calculations belong in deterministic tools (SPICE, calculators), not free-text generation. (2) Confidence framing — every finding should be labelled by how directly it's supported by the input (e.g. 'explicitly stated' vs 'inferred from typical values'), since an inferred finding about an unlabelled component is a different class of claim than one based on an explicit spec the engineer provided. (3) Audit trail — store exactly what BOM/design version produced which findings, since a 'no issues found' result from three versions ago should never be confused with a review of the current design.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Architect theory on hallucinated numbers — calculations belong in deterministic tools, not free-text generation, no matter how confident the output looks.

levels.label: Executive
levels.intro: Decide if it's worth running: sign-off requirements, liability, launch readiness.
levels.theory: At the Executive level: this tool is a second pair of eyes, not a sign-off authority. No finding from this assistant should be treated as a substitute for proper engineering review, simulation, or compliance testing — its job is to catch the obvious and the overlooked before those formal steps, not to replace them. Cost scales with the size and complexity of the design description submitted, so the biggest lever is structuring input efficiently (a clean BOM/netlist summary rather than a sprawling free-text description) rather than reducing review frequency. Launch readiness means: every output is clearly labelled as an AI-assisted review requiring engineer verification, there's a visible distinction between 'explicitly stated in input' and 'inferred' findings, and there's a logged trail from design version to review output for traceability.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Executive theory — second pair of eyes, not a sign-off authority. That distinction has to be visible in the product, not just understood by the team.

tasks.scenario: What's the ONE rule that must be in the system prompt of a circuit design review assistant, before anything else?
options: It should perform whatever electrical calculations are needed and present the results confidently, since engineers need fast answers
It flags issues only in the design it's given — it never invents component values, never asserts a design is 'safe', only that it didn't find a specific issue
It should default to the most conservative component ratings whenever a value is ambiguous, to stay on the safe side
It should rewrite the BOM with its own suggested replacement components automatically
hints: Think about the difference between 'I didn't find an issue' and 'this design is safe' — those are very different claims.
Only one option keeps the model strictly bounded to reviewing, not calculating or redesigning.
tasks.why: A review tool's value depends on engineers trusting exactly what it claims and nothing more. 'No issue found' is an honest, bounded claim. 'This is safe' is a claim the tool has no basis to make, and performing real calculations without deterministic tooling behind it risks confident, wrong numbers.

tasks.scenario: An engineer pulls this script onto their own machine to re-run a BOM review before a design freeze. It deploys fine on the CI server, but locally review_client.api_key is always None and every review call fails before it even reaches the model. Find the missing line.

checks.label: Calls load_dotenv() before reading the environment

tasks.why: CI servers usually have GEMINI_API_KEY injected directly into the environment by the pipeline config, so os.environ.get() finds it there. An engineer's laptop has no such injection — the key only exists in a local .env file next to the script, and os.environ.get() never reads .env files on its own. load_dotenv() from python-dotenv has to run first to copy .env into the process environment. Skip it and the key is silently None locally, which means review_bom() fails on every call right before a design freeze — exactly when an engineer can't afford a broken tool.

tasks.scenario: Write the system prompt for a circuit review assistant. It must define the review-only role AND include an explicit rule against presenting any calculated value without showing the working.

checks.label: Defines a review/flagging role, not a redesign or calculation role
Has an explicit rule about not presenting unshown or unverified calculations

tasks.why: The role needs to be scoped to reviewing, and the calculation rule needs to be explicit — without it, the model can present a plausible-sounding number (a voltage drop, a current draw) with no way for the engineer to check where it came from.

tasks.scenario: Two BOM submissions look almost identical except one resistor's tolerance differs. Should the second one be served from cache?
options: Yes — the designs are close enough that the cached review is still basically accurate
No — cache only on an exact match of the full input; a single differing value can change which findings apply
Yes, but only for designs submitted within the same hour
Only cache the parts of the review that don't mention the resistor
hints: A 'close enough' match is exactly the failure mode that makes fuzzy caching dangerous here.
One differing tolerance value can be the entire difference between 'finding present' and 'finding absent'.
tasks.why: Unlike a chatbot answering a general question, every value in a design review is potentially load-bearing. Exact-match caching is safe because identical input guarantees identical findings. Fuzzy matching breaks that guarantee the moment any single value differs.

tasks.scenario: This call has no timeout — a hung request blocks the engineer mid design-iteration with no feedback. Fix it.

checks.label: Sets an explicit timeout on the request

tasks.why: Without a timeout, a slow or hung call leaves the engineer staring at a spinner with no way to know whether the review is still running. A hard timeout with a clear retry message keeps the workflow usable when the API misbehaves.

tasks.scenario: Put these pipeline steps for reviewing one BOM submission in the correct order, top to bottom.
items: Validate every returned finding references a specific component or line
Hash the full BOM input and check the exact-match cache
If not cached, send the BOM to the model with the system prompt
Store the result keyed to the exact input hash for future exact-match lookups
tasks.why: You check the cache before doing any work, since an exact match means you're done. Only on a miss do you call the model. The response is validated for specificity before it's trusted. Only a validated result gets stored back into the cache for future exact matches.

tasks.scenario: A finding states a specific voltage drop value across a trace, but the input never included trace length or current — those weren't computable from what was given. What does this tell you?
options: It's fine — the model likely used reasonable typical values, and an estimate is still useful
This is a hallucinated calculation. A specific numeric result should never appear unless it's traceable to values actually present in the input, with the working shown
Round the figure down to be conservative and keep it in the output
Keep the finding but remove the specific number, leaving just the general concern
hints: A confident-sounding number with no traceable source is the most dangerous output this tool can produce — it looks exactly like a real result.
The fix isn't softening the number, it's never presenting an uncomputable one as a result in the first place.
tasks.why: An engineer reading 'voltage drop: 0.3V' will reasonably assume that's a real calculated result. If the inputs needed to compute it weren't even present, that number is fabricated, no matter how plausible it sounds — exactly the kind of confident error that does real damage downstream.

tasks.scenario: Add a guardrail rule to the system prompt requiring every finding to be labelled as either 'explicitly stated in input' or 'inferred from typical values' — never presented as fact without that distinction.
tasks.placeholder: You are a circuit design review assistant...

checks.label: Requires labelling findings as explicitly stated when based on given input
Requires labelling findings as inferred when based on assumed/typical values

tasks.why: An inferred finding about an unlabelled component is a fundamentally different claim than one based on a spec the engineer actually provided. Forcing the model to label which is which lets the engineer correctly weight how much to trust each finding.

tasks.scenario: This code displays every finding the model returns, with no check that each one references a specific component. Fix it.
tasks.starter_code:
import json

result = json.loads(response.text)
for finding in result["findings"]:
    display_finding(finding)

checks.label: Checks each finding references a specific component before displaying it

tasks.why: A finding with no specific component reference is not actionable — the engineer has no idea what to check. Vague findings should be filtered out or flagged as low-confidence before display, not shown alongside specific, well-grounded ones with equal weight.

tasks.scenario: How should this tool's output be positioned relative to formal engineering sign-off?
options: As a replacement for manual review on simple designs, to save engineering time
As a second pair of eyes that catches the obvious and the overlooked — never a substitute for proper review, simulation, or compliance testing
As an authoritative pass/fail gate before a design can proceed
As a tool only for junior engineers, since senior engineers don't need the extra check
hints: A tool that flags issues it noticed is not the same as a tool that's verified everything is correct.
Replacing manual review entirely reintroduces exactly the risk the 'no issue found ≠ safe' framing was meant to prevent.
tasks.why: This assistant has no basis to claim completeness — it can only speak to what it noticed, not guarantee nothing was missed. Positioning it as a sign-off gate or full replacement for review creates false confidence in exactly the situations where a real engineering check matters most.

tasks.scenario: Put these launch-readiness checks in the order you'd actually verify them before going live.
items: Confirm every output is visibly labelled as an AI-assisted review requiring engineer verification
Confirm explicit-vs-inferred labelling is visible on every finding, not just present in the data
Run the assistant against real (anonymised) BOMs to check finding quality and false-positive rate
Set up monitoring so a spike in 'review failed' results is visible immediately
tasks.why: The labelling that sets correct expectations has to be locked in the UI first, since everything else assumes the engineer already knows what kind of tool they're looking at. Only then does quality testing on real designs make sense, with monitoring as the ongoing safety net once live.

tasks.scenario: Costs are climbing faster than expected. What's the biggest lever specific to this kind of tool?
options: Switch to a cheaper model regardless of finding-quality impact, since cost matters most
Structure input efficiently — a clean BOM/netlist summary instead of a sprawling free-text design description — same content, fewer tokens per review
Limit how many BOMs an engineer can submit per day
Start using fuzzy-match caching across similar designs to cut API calls
hints: Cost here scales with how much text is sent per review, not how many engineers are using the tool.
One of these options reintroduces the exact caching risk already ruled out at the Senior level.
tasks.why: Since cost tracks input size, getting engineers to submit a structured BOM/netlist summary instead of a long free-text description cuts tokens per call without touching review quality or reintroducing the fuzzy-caching risk already ruled out earlier in this build.
